mnemonics: Base Pointer, Derived Object (BPDO): A base class pointer can point to a derived object, but it can only access base class members unless you use virtual functions.
keyPoints: A pointer to a base class can point to an object of any class derived from that base class.
Access through a base pointer is limited to members defined in the base class, unless those functions are declared 'virtual'.
This principle is the foundation for achieving runtime polymorphism in C++.
commonMistakes: Trying to access a derived-class-specific member using a base class pointer without a proper cast (like dynamic_cast).
Forgetting to declare destructors as 'virtual' in the base class, which can lead to resource leaks when deleting a derived object through a base pointer.
